title: Cleft Lip and Palate: Comprehensive Management
objectives: Understand the embryology of facial development and cleft formation
Classify clefts according to anatomical involvement

sections.title: Epidemiology
sections.content:
**Most common craniofacial anomaly**

• Cleft lip ± palate: 1/700 births
• Isolated cleft palate: 1/2,500 births
• Male predominance for CL±P
• Female predominance for isolated CP

**Risk Factors:**
• Genetic factors (multifactorial)
• Maternal smoking, alcohol
• Anticonvulsants (phenytoin, valproate)
• Folate deficiency
• Family history (30-40x increased risk with affected sibling)

sections.title: Embryology
sections.content:
**Normal Facial Development (Weeks 4-10):**

**Primary Palate (Lip + Anterior Palate):**
• Fusion of medial nasal prominences
• Completed by week 6
• Failure → Cleft lip

**Secondary Palate (Hard + Soft Palate):**
• Fusion of palatine shelves
• Completed by weeks 7-10
• Failure → Cleft palate

**Key Concept:** Lip and palate form separately - can have isolated defects or both

sections.title: Classification
sections.content:
**Cleft Lip:**
• Unilateral vs bilateral
• Complete (to nostril floor) vs incomplete
• Simonart's band (tissue bridge)

**Cleft Palate:**
• Primary palate (anterior to incisive foramen)
• Secondary palate (posterior to incisive foramen)
• Complete vs incomplete
• Submucous cleft (occult - bifid uvula, zona pellucida)

**Combined:**
• Unilateral complete cleft lip and palate (most common)
• Bilateral complete cleft lip and palate

sections.title: Associated Problems
sections.content:
**Feeding:**
• Cannot generate negative pressure
• Nasal regurgitation
• Special bottles/nipples required

**Hearing:**
• Eustachian tube dysfunction
• Chronic otitis media with effusion
• PE tubes often needed

**Speech:**
• Velopharyngeal insufficiency
• Hypernasal speech
• Articulation errors

**Dental:**
• Missing/malformed teeth
• Malocclusion
• Alveolar bone grafting needed

**Psychosocial:**
• Appearance concerns
• Bullying
• Support essential

sections.title: Multidisciplinary Care Timeline
sections.content:
**Rule of 10s for Lip Repair:**
• 10 weeks old
• 10 pounds weight
• Hemoglobin 10 g/dL

**Surgical Timeline:**
• 3-6 months: Lip repair
• 9-12 months: Palate repair
• 8-11 years: Alveolar bone graft
• Adolescence: Rhinoplasty, orthognathic surgery

**Team Members:**
• Plastic/craniofacial surgery
• ENT
• Speech pathology
• Audiology
• Orthodontics
• Genetics
• Social work/psychology

sections.title: Syndromes & Sequences
sections.content:
**Pierre Robin Sequence:**
• Micrognathia → Glossoptosis → Airway obstruction + Cleft palate

**Van der Woude Syndrome:**
• Cleft lip/palate + lip pits
• Autosomal dominant (IRF6)

**Velocardiofacial (22q11):**
• Cleft palate, cardiac defects, immune deficiency
• CATCH-22

**Stickler Syndrome:**
• Cleft palate, myopia, joint problems
• Collagen disorder

**When to Suspect Syndrome:**
• Isolated cleft palate (30% syndromic)
• Other anomalies
• Family history
• Genetics referral recommended

keyTakeaways: Cleft lip and palate form from different embryologic processes
Isolated cleft palate has higher rate of syndromic association
Feeding is the immediate concern - special techniques required
Rule of 10s guides timing of lip repair
Hearing and speech require ongoing monitoring

reviewQuestions.question: A newborn is noted to have a bifid uvula and a palpable notch in the hard palate, but mucosa appears intact. What is this condition?
reviewQuestions.explanation: Submucous cleft palate is characterized by the classic triad: bifid uvula, zona pellucida (thin midline mucosa), and notched hard palate. It can cause velopharyngeal insufficiency and requires monitoring for speech development.

mcqs.stem: Which of the following is the MOST important initial step in evaluating a patient with suspected cleft lip palate?

options.text: Obtain a thorough history and physical examination
options.explanation: A comprehensive history and physical examination is the foundation of clinical reasoning in cleft lip palate. It guides all subsequent diagnostic and management decisions.
Advanced imaging may be indicated but should be guided by clinical findings, not ordered reflexively.
Treatment should generally follow an appropriate diagnostic evaluation unless the patient is critically unstable.

options.text: Consult a specialist before any workup
options.explanation: Initial evaluation should be performed by the treating physician; specialist consultation follows based on findings.

mcqs.stem: A patient presents with classic features of cleft lip palate. Which clinical reasoning principle is MOST important in avoiding diagnostic error?

options.text: Search satisficing - stop looking after the first diagnosis
options.explanation: Premature closure (stopping the diagnostic process too early) is a common cognitive error that leads to missed diagnoses.
Systematic consideration of differential diagnoses, including must-not-miss conditions, is essential for avoiding premature closure and anchoring bias.
While pattern recognition (System 1 thinking) is useful, it should be balanced with analytical reasoning (System 2) to avoid cognitive biases.

options.text: Order every available test to rule out all possibilities
options.explanation: Indiscriminate testing leads to false positives, unnecessary procedures, and increased cost without improving diagnostic accuracy.

mcqs.stem: When managing cleft lip palate, which approach best supports evidence-based practice?

options.text: Follow institutional protocols regardless of patient factors
options.explanation: Evidence-based practice integrates best evidence with clinical expertise AND patient values/circumstances.

options.text: Base decisions solely on clinical experience
options.explanation: Clinical experience is valuable but can introduce bias; it should be combined with current evidence.

options.text: Integrate current best evidence with clinical expertise and patient values
options.explanation: Evidence-based medicine is the conscientious integration of best research evidence, clinical expertise, and patient values in making clinical decisions.

options.text: Always defer to the most recent published study
options.explanation: Individual studies should be appraised for quality and relevance; systematic reviews and guidelines generally provide stronger evidence.
